data.title: The Cargo Run Gone Sideways
data.premise: You're hired to transport a shipment of 'agricultural equipment' to a backwater moon, but the cargo turns out to be something far more complicated—and the buyer might not be who they claim. Now you've got Alliance inspectors, a jilted crime lord, and your own crew's questions to dodge before the job goes completely south.
data.central_question: Can you deliver the cargo, keep your crew safe, and figure out who's actually pulling the strings before everything explodes?
data.ending_shape: escape achieved with a twist revealed
starting_inventory: Firefly's flight controls (mental map of ship systems)
A well-used pistol with three rounds
A personal letter from the job broker (now destroyed, but you remember the basics)

setting.name: Eavesdown Docks and the moon Meridian
setting.description: You're docked at a sprawling spaceport on the edge of the 'verse, where independent traders, smugglers, and desperate folks converge. The moon Meridian itself is a frontier settlement—dust, heat, and the kind of place where nobody asks questions if you don't want them to. Alliance patrols are sparse but unpredictable.
setting.tone_notes: Witty, scrappy, slightly chaotic. Danger and humor coexist. Think improvisation under pressure, clever dialogue, and the constant low hum of something about to go wrong in a way that's almost funny. The tone should feel like watching Mal try to talk his way out of a situation he's already half-way into.

acts.title: The Job Looks Easy
acts.description: You meet with the cargo broker at a seedy cantina. The deal seems straightforward: transport sealed crates to Meridian, get paid, move on. But the broker is nervous, the amount offered is too good, and your crew is already suspicious. You load the cargo without opening it (per contract) and prepare to launch.
acts.end_condition: Firefly leaves dock and enters atmo for Meridian
locked_constraints: The cargo cannot be opened yet
key_beats: Meeting the nervous broker and learning details (or not)
Kaylee warns you the deal smells wrong
An Alliance patrol ship passes nearby but doesn't hail
You make the call to launch

acts.title: Everything Gets Complicated
acts.description: En route to Meridian, you receive a coded message: the cargo's real buyer is not who the broker claimed. Worse, an Alliance inspection team has been dispatched to Meridian to intercept 'smuggled medical supplies.' Simultaneously, a ship matching a known crime lord's vessel is on an intercept course. You have to decide: land anyway, divert, or try to outrun both threats. Meanwhile, Simon discovers the cargo contains something he recognizes—and it terrifies him.
acts.end_condition: You make a definitive choice about landing on Meridian or diverting
locked_constraints: You cannot reach Alliance space safely
The crime lord's ship cannot be outrun indefinitely
Simon's discovery must complicate your decision
key_beats: Coded message arrives; real buyer revealed
Crime lord's ship detected on scope
Simon pulls you aside with his discovery
You commit to a course of action

acts.title: The Bluff
acts.description: You execute your plan—whether that's landing under false transponder codes, running a complex bluff with the Alliance, or negotiating with the crime lord. The truth emerges: the cargo is a shipment of black-market medical tech meant to help the outer colonies, and the broker set you up as an unwitting courier. The real conspiracy reaches higher than anyone expected. You escape Meridian with the cargo, the crew, and a new understanding of who your enemies really are—and who might be your unexpected allies.
acts.end_condition: Firefly escapes Meridian's gravity well; Alliance/crime lord threat neutralized or evaded
locked_constraints: Mal cannot willingly side with the Alliance
At least one crew member must have doubts about the final choice
key_beats: Execution of the chosen escape plan begins
A betrayal or unexpected alliance shifts the situation
The real purpose of the cargo is revealed
A moment of genuine danger (firefight, system failure, etc.)
A clever or audacious move gets you clear
Escape achieved; crew processes what just happened

npcs.name: Zoe Alleyne Washburne
npcs.role: first mate and voice of reason
npcs.motivation: Protect the crew and keep Mal from doing something monumentally stupid
npcs.secret: She's already figured out the cargo is medical tech and approved of the mission before Mal did

npcs.name: Kaylee Frye
npcs.motivation: Keep Serenity flying and make sure Mal doesn't hurt innocent people
npcs.secret: She overheard the broker on the dock and has been worried the whole time

npcs.name: Simon Tam
npcs.motivation: Protect River and expose the real medical conspiracy
npcs.secret: He recognizes the cargo as coming from the same black-site facility that experimented on River

npcs.name: River Tam
npcs.motivation: Help her brother and feel useful despite her fragmented mind
npcs.secret: Her instincts about the cargo are 100% correct, but nobody fully understands her warnings

npcs.name: Hoban 'Wash' Washburne
npcs.role: pilot and comic relief under pressure
npcs.motivation: Fly the ship and keep everyone alive through sheer skill and wit
npcs.secret: He's already plotted three alternative escape routes without telling anyone

npcs.name: Derrial Book
npcs.role: spiritual advisor with a hidden past
npcs.motivation: Ensure the medical supplies reach people who need them
npcs.secret: He has prior knowledge that the Alliance is searching for this exact cargo

npcs.name: Inara Serra
npcs.motivation: Maintain her professional reputation while helping the crew
npcs.secret: She has contacts on Meridian who can facilitate the delivery if things go south

npcs.name: Marcus Crane
npcs.motivation: Complete the transaction without getting caught or killed
npcs.secret: He was hired by a third party to hire Mal, and he doesn't know the full truth either

npcs.name: Commander Vex Harrow
npcs.motivation: Intercept the cargo and arrest whoever's transporting it
npcs.secret: She's been tipped off by someone inside the conspiracy, but she doesn't know who her real target is

npcs.name: Kess Venn
npcs.motivation: Recover the cargo and punish anyone who stole from him
npcs.secret: He's being played by the same conspiracy that hired Mal—he thinks the cargo is his, but it was never actually his

npcs.name: Dr. Elena Vasquez
npcs.motivation: Get the medical tech to outer colony settlements where people are dying
npcs.secret: She orchestrated the entire job and set Mal up as a patsy, but for genuinely good reasons

factions.name: Alliance
factions.description: The central government seeking to control and monopolize medical technology in the outer rim
factions.goals: Intercept the cargo and maintain their monopoly on advanced medical supplies

factions.name: Outer Rim Independence Movement
factions.description: A loose network of colonists, doctors, and rebels working to supply remote settlements with life-saving technology
factions.goals: Distribute the medical tech to settlements where people are dying from treatable conditions

world_rules: The Alliance controls major trade routes but has limited enforcement in the outer rim
Cargo contracts are legally binding and breaking them results in a bounty on your head
Communication delays in space mean decisions made now won't be questioned for hours
Crime lords in the 'verse always have leverage—direct confrontation rarely ends well
Black-market medical tech is incredibly valuable and dangerous in the wrong hands
Firefly is fast but not invulnerable; sustained pursuit will eventually catch you

data.passagePreview: The Eavesdown Docks smell like rust, recycled air, and desperation—which is to say, they smell like

data.passage:
The Eavesdown Docks smell like rust, recycled air, and desperation—which is to say, they smell like home. You stand on Serenity's cargo ramp, squinting against the afternoon glare bleeding through the station's upper hatches, watching a nervous man in a sweat-stained coat hurry toward you. That's Marcus Crane, your job broker. Behind you, Zoe leans against the ramp's frame with her arms crossed, already looking like she's mentally calculating exit strategies.

"Captain Reynolds," Crane says, breathless. He's clutching a datapad like it might explode. "Good, good. You're still here. I was worried you'd—well. Never mind. The cargo's ready for loading. All sealed. Per the contract, you don't open it. You don't look at it. You just deliver it to Meridian and collect your 2,500 credits."

Zoe shifts slightly. You can feel her skepticism from here.

"That's a lot of credits for hauling freight that smells like it's already going bad," you say carefully. Crane's eye twitches. Perfect tell. "What exactly are we transporting, Marcus?"

"Agricultural equipment," he says too quickly. "Perfectly legal. The manifest is vague on purpose—standard practice for sensitive trade routes."

From inside the ship, you hear Kaylee's voice: "Captain! We've got a problem with the fuel coupling!" Whether that's real or a convenient interruption is anyone's guess, but Crane's already sweating through his collar.

You've got maybe five minutes to make a decision before this gets more complicated.

A.text: Press Crane for specifics. If the cargo is legal, he should be able to tell you what's actually in those crates. You didn't survive this long by flying blind.

B.text: Accept the job as-is and get the cargo loaded immediately. The less you know, the less you can be forced to admit if things go south. Zoe can oversee the loading while you help Kaylee with the fuel coupling.

C.text: Tell Crane you need to talk to your crew first—specifically, pull Zoe, Simon, and Book aside to get their read on this situation before you commit. Something feels wrong, and you want their eyes on it.

D.text: Excuse yourself and walk the docks for five minutes. Get eyes on Crane from a distance—watch who he meets with, who he's talking to on his comm. If this job has teeth, you want to know whose teeth they are before you get bitten.

PASSAGE:
The Eavesdown Docks smell like rust, recycled air, and desperation—which is to say, they smell like home. You stand on Serenity's cargo ramp, squinting against the afternoon glare bleeding through the station's upper hatches, watching a nervous man in a sweat-stained coat hurry toward you. That's Marcus Crane, your job broker. Behind you, Zoe leans against the ramp's frame with her arms crossed, already looking like she's mentally calculating exit strategies.

"Captain Reynolds," Crane says, breathless. He's clutching a datapad like it might explode. "Good, good. You're still here. I was worried you'd—well. Never mind. The cargo's ready for loading. All sealed. Per the contract, you don't open it. You don't look at it. You just deliver it to Meridian and collect your 2,500 credits."

Zoe shifts slightly. You can feel her skepticism from here.

"That's a lot of credits for hauling freight that smells like it's already going bad," you say carefully. Crane's eye twitches. Perfect tell. "What exactly are we transporting, Marcus?"

"Agricultural equipment," he says too quickly. "Perfectly legal. The manifest is vague on purpose—standard practice for sensitive trade routes."

From inside the ship, you hear Kaylee's voice: "Captain! We've got a problem with the fuel coupling!" Whether that's real or a convenient interruption is anyone's guess, but Crane's already sweating through his collar.

You've got maybe five minutes to make a decision before this gets more complicated.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Press Crane for specifics. If the cargo is legal, he should be able to tell you what's actually in those crates. You didn't survive this long by flying blind.
B: Accept the job as-is and get the cargo loaded immediately. The less you know, the less you can be forced to admit if things go south. Zoe can oversee the loading while you help Kaylee with the fuel coupling.
C: Tell Crane you need to talk to your crew first—specifically, pull Zoe, Simon, and Book aside to get their read on this situation before you commit. Something feels wrong, and you want their eyes on it.
D: Excuse yourself and walk the docks for five minutes. Get eyes on Crane from a distance—watch who he meets with, who he's talking to on his comm. If this job has teeth, you want to know whose teeth they are before you get bitten.

data.passage:
You nod at Zoe, already moving toward the ship's belly. "Load it up. I want Serenity breathing vacuum in two hours." She doesn't ask questions—that's what you pay her for—and wheels toward the dock workers with the manifest.

Kaylee's waiting by the starboard coupling, wrench in hand, grease already under her fingernails. "Cap, the pressure valve's been weeping again," she says, not looking up. "Nothing critical, but if we push hard on the burn, we might lose efficiency."

You crouch beside her, the familiar work settling something in your chest. Behind you, the cargo crates are being loaded—heavy, unmarked boxes that thud against Serenity's deck plating with a finality that makes your jaw tighten. You don't look. Zoe's got it.

"How long to fix?" you ask.

"Twenty minutes if you hand me things. Thirty if I'm solo." Kaylee finally meets your eyes, and you catch something there—worry, maybe, or a question she's learned not to ask.

From the cargo hold, River's voice drifts up, singing something wordless and off-key. Simon's murmuring to her. Wash is already in the cockpit running pre-flight. Book's checking manifest details with Zoe. Inara's nowhere to be seen—probably in her shuttle, staying professional distance from dock labor.

The afternoon sun reflects off the tarmac, and for a moment, everything feels ordinary. Routine. Safe.

Then your cortex unit crackles with a priority frequency you don't recognize, and someone official-sounding begins: "All vessels in Eavesdown sector, be advised—Alliance inspection sweep commencing in forty minutes..."

A.text: Kill the cortex unit transmission immediately and get Wash to start the launch sequence now. The Alliance can't inspect what's already in atmo.

B.text: Stay calm, finish the fuel coupling with Kaylee, and have Wash respond to the Alliance with standard dock protocols. Acting suspicious is what gets you caught.

C.text: Tell Kaylee to abandon the coupling and grab Zoe—pull the cargo crew and make a scene about a mechanical emergency. Launch under the cover of chaos.

D.text: Head to the cortex unit and try to get more details about the sweep parameters. Maybe it's routine, maybe it's not. You need information before you move.

You nod at Zoe, already moving toward the ship's belly. "Load it up. I want Serenity breathing vacuum in two hours." She doesn't ask questions—that's what you pay her for—and wheels toward the dock workers with the manifest.

Kaylee's waiting by the starboard coupling, wrench in hand, grease already under her fingernails. "Cap, the pressure valve's been weeping again," she says, not looking up. "Nothing critical, but if we push hard on the burn, we might lose efficiency."

You crouch beside her, the familiar work settling something in your chest. Behind you, the cargo crates are being loaded—heavy, unmarked boxes that thud against Serenity's deck plating with a finality that makes your jaw tighten. You don't look. Zoe's got it.

"How long to fix?" you ask.

"Twenty minutes if you hand me things. Thirty if I'm solo." Kaylee finally meets your eyes, and you catch something there—worry, maybe, or a question she's learned not to ask.

From the cargo hold, River's voice drifts up, singing something wordless and off-key. Simon's murmuring to her. Wash is already in the cockpit running pre-flight. Book's checking manifest details with Zoe. Inara's nowhere to be seen—probably in her shuttle, staying professional distance from dock labor.

The afternoon sun reflects off the tarmac, and for a moment, everything feels ordinary. Routine. Safe.

data.passage:
You climb topside to the bridge, where Wash is running pre-flight checks with the kind of focused intensity that means he's already nervous. The cortex hums its usual steady note, and you pull up the Alliance frequency logs—nothing classified, just the routine chatter you're supposed to ignore.

Except it's not routine.

The sweep parameters came through forty minutes ago, but there's a second transmission buried underneath, timestamped three hours earlier. It's encrypted, but not heavily—someone wanted it found by the right person. You crack it in under two minutes. The text is sparse: "Verify cargo manifest. Inspect Firefly specifically. Report contents directly to Commander Harrow, not local dispatch."

Your stomach tightens. Harrow is Alliance Intelligence, not customs. She doesn't inspect cargo. She investigates threats.

Wash notices your expression. "Cap, you're doing that thing where your jaw gets all tight. That's the face you make right before someone shoots at us."

Before you can answer, Book appears in the doorway. "Mal, we've got a problem. Zoe's down in the bay with Kaylee. One of the crates—the small sealed unit in the back—it's warm. Actively warm. Whatever's in there is running on internal power."

Your mind snaps into focus. This isn't a routine sweep. It's a trap, and you walked Serenity right into it. The job broker knew. Maybe he was forced, maybe he didn't care. But someone at Alliance command level wanted to know exactly what you were carrying, and they wanted you caught with it in dock.

You've got thirty minutes before Harrow's team arrives.

A.text: Abort the loading immediately. Get the crew to stations, cast off now, and jump atmo before the inspectors arrive. Better to run than get caught in dock.

B.text: Contact Zoe and Simon—get them to that warm crate and find out what's actually in there. You need intel before you make any moves.

C.text: Reach out to Marcus Crane. He set this up, whether he meant to or not. Force him to tell you who really hired him and what Harrow actually knows.

D.text: Shut down the cortex logs, wipe the encrypted transmission, and prep a false manifest. If Harrow wants to inspect, let her—but she'll find exactly what the paperwork says she'll find.

  },

    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "1-2 sentences — what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "1-2 sentences — direction for Writer, not a script" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

FIELD LENGTH RULES:
- consequence: 1-2 sentences. State the mechanical effect only — what changes, what is lost/gained.
- narration_directive: 1-2 sentences. Give the Writer a direction, not a script. Say WHAT happens, not HOW to write it.
- event_log_entry: One sentence.
- new_scene_context: 1-2 sentences.

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

BRUTAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- The world is hostile. Consequences are immediate and devastating. When a choice goes wrong, it goes VERY wrong.
- Consequences compound and do not resolve on their own. The danger comes from the world being unforgiving, not from artificially limiting safe options — let the narrative situation dictate how many choices are risky on any given turn.
- TRAP LOGIC (Brutal-exclusive): Some choices that APPEAR safe MUST actually carry hidden severe consequences. Base these on earlier context the player may have missed — an NPC's hidden motive, a world flag they ignored, a warning sign in a previous passage. The narration_directive for trap choices MUST instruct the Writer to reveal the trap dramatically.
- game_over outcomes SHOULD appear regularly — whenever the narrative situation creates genuine danger, at least one choice should be lethal/fatal/catastrophic.
- Setbacks MUST cascade. If the player is already in trouble, make the situation worse. Do NOT offer easy recovery.
- NPCs NEVER forgive. One wrong interaction permanently closes that relationship. Relationship penalties are large (-40 or worse) and irreversible.
- narration_directive: be brutal and specific. Tell the Writer to show the full weight of consequences with no softening, no last-minute saves, no silver linings.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option D.
Pre-classified outcome: HIDDEN_BENEFIT
Pre-determined consequence: "You reach the cortex unit and learn the sweep is routine—random cargo verification, no specific targets. This intelligence lets you relax and proceed normally. However, while monitoring the frequency, you overhear a secondary encrypted channel mentioning 'medical contraband' and 'Meridian delivery.' Someone knows. You gain critical information but also confirm the cargo is flagged somewhere in the system."
Narration directive: "Show Mal intercepting the encrypted chatter—a moment of realization that this job is already compromised at a higher level. The routine sweep is cover for something deeper. Make the discovery feel like a trap closing."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative consequences. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You climb topside to the bridge, where Wash is running pre-flight checks with the kind of focused intensity that means he's already nervous. The cortex hums its usual steady note, and you pull up the Alliance frequency logs—nothing classified, just the routine chatter you're supposed to ignore.

Except it's not routine.

The sweep parameters came through forty minutes ago, but there's a second transmission buried underneath, timestamped three hours earlier. It's encrypted, but not heavily—someone wanted it found by the right person. You crack it in under two minutes. The text is sparse: "Verify cargo manifest. Inspect Firefly specifically. Report contents directly to Commander Harrow, not local dispatch."

Your stomach tightens. Harrow is Alliance Intelligence, not customs. She doesn't inspect cargo. She investigates threats.

Wash notices your expression. "Cap, you're doing that thing where your jaw gets all tight. That's the face you make right before someone shoots at us."

Before you can answer, Book appears in the doorway. "Mal, we've got a problem. Zoe's down in the bay with Kaylee. One of the crates—the small sealed unit in the back—it's warm. Actively warm. Whatever's in there is running on internal power."

Your mind snaps into focus. This isn't a routine sweep. It's a trap, and you walked Serenity right into it. The job broker knew. Maybe he was forced, maybe he didn't care. But someone at Alliance command level wanted to know exactly what you were carrying, and they wanted you caught with it in dock.

You've got thirty minutes before Harrow's team arrives.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Abort the loading immediately. Get the crew to stations, cast off now, and jump atmo before the inspectors arrive. Better to run than get caught in dock.
B: Contact Zoe and Simon—get them to that warm crate and find out what's actually in there. You need intel before you make any moves.
C: Reach out to Marcus Crane. He set this up, whether he meant to or not. Force him to tell you who really hired him and what Harrow actually knows.
D: Shut down the cortex logs, wipe the encrypted transmission, and prep a false manifest. If Harrow wants to inspect, let her—but she'll find exactly what the paperwork says she'll find.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

However, the warm crate remains a problem—it will register on thermal scans. You've bought time and plausible deniability, but the crate itself is still a ticking clock that will eventually expose you.
D.narration_directive: Show Mal's cold professionalism as he manipulates the records—a captain who's done this before. Make it feel like a temporary reprieve, not a solution. The crate's heat signature is still there, waiting to betray him.

data.passagePreview: You punch the comm. "All hands, abort cargo ops. Wash, get those engines hot. We're launching now."

data.passage:
You punch the comm. "All hands, abort cargo ops. Wash, get those engines hot. We're launching now."

Zoe doesn't hesitate—she's already moving toward the bridge. Kaylee's voice crackles back, breathless: "Captain, we're not even halfway through securing—" "Leave it," you cut her off. "Go."

The alarm screams to life as you strap into the captain's chair. Wash's hands dance across his console, and Serenity's engines roar from idle to full burn in seconds. The ship shudders violently as cargo containers shift in the bay—something heavy slams against the hold walls. Simon grabs the rail. River watches the viewport with that eerie calm of hers.

Zoe appears at your shoulder. "They're moving. Three Alliance vehicles, breaking toward the dock."

You feel the deck lurch as Wash applies lateral thrust, pulling away from the moorings. Sparks shower across the viewport where the umbilical cord snaps. The docking clamps disengage with a metallic shriek that rattles your teeth.

"Harrow's on comms," Wash says, voice tight. "Demanding we stand down. Says we've got five seconds before they fire."

Serenity screams upward, dust and debris swirling in her wake. Through the viewport, you see Alliance troops scattering, weapons raised but too late. The planet's curve starts pulling away. Your heart hammers against your ribs.

You've just burned every bridge at Eavesdown. There's no coming back from this. Not today.

A.text: Tell Wash to ignore Harrow's threats and punch it straight for atmo. Full throttle. Let them chase you into the black—Serenity's faster in a vertical climb.

B.text: Order Wash to cut engines and raise your hands on open comms. Offer to submit to a search—you haven't opened the cargo, so technically you're clean. Play it smooth.

C.text: Head for the industrial sector of the spaceport—weave between shipping containers and station architecture to break their line of sight. Lose them in the clutter.

D.text: Ask Simon what's in that cargo bay that's worth running this hard. If you're going to burn your reputation, you deserve to know what you're burning it for.

PASSAGE:
You punch the comm. "All hands, abort cargo ops. Wash, get those engines hot. We're launching now."

Zoe doesn't hesitate—she's already moving toward the bridge. Kaylee's voice crackles back, breathless: "Captain, we're not even halfway through securing—" "Leave it," you cut her off. "Go."

The alarm screams to life as you strap into the captain's chair. Wash's hands dance across his console, and Serenity's engines roar from idle to full burn in seconds. The ship shudders violently as cargo containers shift in the bay—something heavy slams against the hold walls. Simon grabs the rail. River watches the viewport with that eerie calm of hers.

Zoe appears at your shoulder. "They're moving. Three Alliance vehicles, breaking toward the dock."

You feel the deck lurch as Wash applies lateral thrust, pulling away from the moorings. Sparks shower across the viewport where the umbilical cord snaps. The docking clamps disengage with a metallic shriek that rattles your teeth.

"Harrow's on comms," Wash says, voice tight. "Demanding we stand down. Says we've got five seconds before they fire."

Serenity screams upward, dust and debris swirling in her wake. Through the viewport, you see Alliance troops scattering, weapons raised but too late. The planet's curve starts pulling away. Your heart hammers against your ribs.

You've just burned every bridge at Eavesdown. There's no coming back from this. Not today.

data.passage:
You should've launched. That's the thought that crystallizes in your skull as Commander Harrow's boots hit your bridge deck, her Alliance-issue sidearm trained on your chest with the casual precision of someone who's ended this conversation a hundred times before. The corridor behind her fills with blue-gloved soldiers, their weapons sweeping across your crew like searchlights hunting rabbits in tall grass.

Simon stands frozen beside the cargo manifest terminal, his mouth still open on the answer you demanded. "Medical technology," he'd started to say, and those two words cost you everything.

You watch Zoe's jaw clench as Harrow's team forces her to her knees. Wash's hands are raised so high his shoulders shake. Kaylee's down in the cargo bay—you can hear the commotion through the comlink, boots on grating, the industrial whine of the cargo bay doors being manually overridden. They're taking Serenity apart, piece by piece, crate by crate.

"Captain Reynolds," Harrow says, and there's something almost apologetic in her tone, like she's sorry your curiosity got in the way of her quota. "You're under arrest for smuggling restricted Alliance medical materials to unauthorized recipients. Your vessel is impounded. Your crew will be remanded for questioning."

You could've made atmo. Wash had the numbers—you heard him calculating the burn just before you killed the launch sequence to drag Simon into the ready room. Thirty seconds. That's all you needed. Thirty seconds and Serenity would've been in the black, beyond their reach, beyond their jurisdiction. But you needed to know. You needed to know what you were risking everything for, and that need—that stubborn, captain's need to understand his own ship—has handed them the whole game.

As they cuff your wrists, you catch Simon's face in the chaos. He looks destroyed, like he's watching someone he knows die slowly. Maybe he is. Your reputation in the 'verse just died in real time, broadcast to every dock, every broker, every captain worth his salt from the Core to the Rim. You're not Malcolm Reynolds anymore. You're that idiot who got caught hauling black-market medical tech because he couldn't shut up and launch.

The irony? You still don't know what's in those crates.
